headline: Momentum is turning up across the board, but every major is still living below its 200-day line
paragraphs: Start with the regime, because it settles most arguments before they begin: all seven majors are trading below their 200-day averages, and not one has a golden cross. Bitcoin sits at $62,927, down 49.6% from its twelve-month high; Ethereum at $1,781 is off 63.1%; Cardano at $0.16 is 83.4% from its peak. That is a bear regime, plainly stated. The 200-day line is the market's long-term memory, and right now the memory of every one of these assets is of higher prices. Nothing in today's tape changes that classification.

What is interesting is what's happening underneath it. Six of the seven majors have MACD above its signal line, the exception is Solana, which means short-term momentum has turned up inside a long-term downtrend. RSI readings back this up: Bitcoin at 48.3, Ethereum at 54.9, Solana at 50.2, all mid-range, neither oversold nor stretched. Dogecoin is the laggard at 34.1. This is the classic profile of a bounce attempt inside a bear regime: momentum firming, trend still pointing down. Which force wins is not for the tape to say today, but the disagreement between the MACD boards and the 200-day boards is the day's first genuine finding.

Positioning is where it gets uneven. Funding is the periodic payment perpetual traders make to hold their side of the bet, positive means longs pay shorts, negative the reverse. Bitcoin funding is essentially flat at -0.1% annualized, and XRP longs are actually being paid at -2.8%, which tells you leverage in the two biggest boats is neither crowded nor expensive. Then there's Dogecoin: 11% annualized funding, a long/short ratio of 3.48 accounts long for every one short, on a coin down 75% from its high with an RSI of 34. That is a small boat, heavily leaning one way, paying rent to stay there. Liquidations over the past 24 hours skewed toward longs on Bitcoin ($10.6M against $2.0M in shorts), consistent with a -1.29% day.

Flows are the day's second tension. The whale ledger over 72 hours shows a modest $10.3M net moving onto exchanges, barely a lean, but the recent weekly sequence matters more: two weeks of heavy off-exchange movement worth $1.57B and $1.41B, then $310M, and now roughly flat-to-negative. The accumulation impulse has cooled. Meanwhile the biggest single moves cut both ways: $113.8M of BTC onto OKX and $96.2M onto Coinbase institutional, against $96.0M leaving Binance and $75.0M leaving Coinbase. On the demand side, the traditional-finance bid is still present, US spot ETFs took in a net $90.4M of Bitcoin and $18.4M of Ethereum on the latest print, but the stablecoin float, the market's dry powder, has shrunk 1.41% over 30 days to $308.8B. Buyers are showing up; the reservoir behind them is not refilling.

Sentiment is the foil, and it's a subtle one. Fear & Greed reads 28, labeled Fear, but that is the very top of its 30-day range of 11 to 28. The crowd was terrified; now it's merely nervous. Set that against a Bitcoin network where the fastest confirmation costs 3 sat/vB and difficulty just ticked up 0.4%, and you have a market that is quiet, cheap to use, and emotionally thawing while structurally still underwater. What to watch: whether Bitcoin can reclaim its 200-day line, which would change the regime classification outright; whether the whale weekly flows resume the off-exchange pattern of the prior two weeks or confirm the flip; and whether Dogecoin's 11% funding cools or that crowded long boat gets tested. Those three readings, more than any headline, will tell you if today's picture held.
